problem_summary: 2017 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability / "Smart Growth"). The team had to: (1) design a metric to measure the smart growth of a city using the 3 E's of sustainability and/or the 10 principles of smart growth; (2) pick two mid-sized cities (pop. 100k-500k) on different continents and evaluate how their current growth plans meet smart-growth principles; (3) build a growth plan for both cities over the next few decades, justify the chosen components, and evaluate the plan; (4) rank individual plan components by potential and compare the two cities; (5) explain how the plan supports an additional 50% population increase by 2050. This team chose Edinburgh (UK) and Wellington (NZ).
models_used: Three-dimensional 'Baseline Cube Model' — a 3D vector-space metric where a city's growth state S = (EDI, SJI, EPI) is a point in a unit cube; the ideal 'smart growth' direction is the cube diagonal (1,1,1)

Vector-geometry Smart Growth Index (SGI): SGI = [cos<CG,SG> + mu*|CG|] / (1 + mu*sqrt(3)), combining direction (cosine of angle between current-growth vector and the ideal diagonal) and speed (length of growth vector), with policy weight mu and sqrt(3)-based normalization to [0,1]

Three composite sub-indices, each a fixed-weight aggregate of normalized indicators: EDI = (1/3)(Life Expectancy + GDP per capita + Expense %GDP); SJI = (3/7)Gini + (2/7)Study Participants + (2/7)Employment Rate (weights borrowed from the published Social Justice Index); EPI = (1/3)(GHG Emissions + Forest Area + Renewable Energy Consumption)

'Modified Cube Model' — a constrained nonlinear program: maximize SGI subject to budget non-negativity, ΔS ≥ 0, and a fixed total-budget equality, over a 7-component budget allocation vector B (transport, social, environmental, education, health, development, other), solved in MATLAB
Linear state-transition / response matrix b (3x7) mapping budget allocation B to change in state ΔS = b·B, used both to forecast future states and to optimize allocation
Ordinary Least Squares estimation of the b matrix from k years of historical budget and state data: b_hat = (B_k^T B_k)^(-1) B_k ΔS_k
Forward-difference discrete dynamics: ΔS_t = S_{t+1} - S_t as a proxy for derivatives dS/dt to roll the state forward over a 30-year horizon
F-test (regression MSR/MSE) for regression significance, and one-at-a-time sensitivity analysis (perturbing Edinburgh's Gini coefficient by 25%)
key_techniques: Reframing a fuzzy policy concept (the 3 E's of sustainability) as a geometric object: turning 'is this smart growth?' into a measurable angle and length in a 3D cube — direction = how balanced/aligned growth is, length = how fast

Two-part objective with an interpretable tunable weight mu, with explicit regimes (mu < 1/sqrt(3) = developed city prioritizing direction; mu > 1/sqrt(3) = developing city prioritizing rate; mu = 1/sqrt(3) = balanced), giving policymakers a knob
Hierarchical three-stage index design (SGI ← EDI/SJI/EPI ← 9 raw indicators) with a clean parameter table, so the metric is auditable top-to-bottom
Min(EDI,SJI,EPI) > 0 as a binary 'smart vs non-smart growth' gate, capturing the idea that sustainability fails on its weakest leg (Liebig's-barrel logic)

Closing the loop from metric to policy: the same cube model is reused as the optimization engine, so the recommended budget reallocation automatically maximizes the metric — diagnosis and prescription share one framework
Data-driven parameter estimation (OLS on 2009-2015 budget vs. state changes) rather than assumed coefficients, grounding the b matrix in each city's real history
Component ranking by |Δ| between current and optimal budget shares to convert the optimization output into a prioritized, comparable initiative list across cities
Qualitative scenario analysis for the 50% population shock: argues SJI/EDI stay roughly fixed (new population mirrors current ratios) while EPI degrades, isolating environmental budget as the lever
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective the paper wins on a single memorable, original modeling idea executed end-to-end. The cube-and-vector metric is visually intuitive, mathematically clean, and directly answers the prompt's hardest task (designing a metric) by collapsing the 3 E's into angle (balance/direction) + length (speed) + a min() sustainability gate. Crucially the SAME model is reused as a constrained optimizer, so the team's diagnosis (Edinburgh is low-level smart growth; Wellington is non-smart growth with strong EDI but negative EPI/SJI) flows seamlessly into a concrete, percentage-level budget reallocation plan, a 30-year forecast, a ranked component comparison, and a population-shock analysis — every numbered task is answered with the one framework. The team also did real homework: two genuine cities on different continents with real census/budget data, OLS-fitted coefficients from 7 years of history, plus F-test and sensitivity validation. The writing maps each restated task to a model section, and the strength/weakness section is unusually honest (admits difference-for-derivative and disaster blindness). It is the combination of a novel-yet-simple central metaphor, full task coverage, real data, and tidy presentation that separates an O-paper here.
section_highlights: Summary Sheet: tight and complete — states the budget assumption, names the cube and its three axes, explains the angle-as-sustainability / length-as-speed intuition up front, and previews every result (two cities, 30-yr forecast, 50% population case, F-test + sensitivity). A judge knows the whole arc before page 3. Restatement of the Problem: each of the 5 official tasks is restated AND immediately paired with the specific model artifact that answers it (cube model, component matrix B, ranking, etc.) — an explicit requirements-to-solution map. Models chapter: builds bottom-up with a clean three-stage parameter table, defines SGI with a summary table breaking the formula into term/description/meaning, and uses the μ-regime figure to make the weight choice concrete. Case Study: real numbers for Edinburgh and Wellington (state tables, CG vectors, SGI = 0.2814 for Edinburgh, non-smart for Wellington), current-vs-optimal budget tables with Δ columns, and forecast line charts per city. Strength/Weakness: organized and candid (Inclusive / Quantification / Simple-but-Universal / Visible vs. Accuracy-relies-on-statistics / Disasters-not-included / Difference-replaced-derivatives).
pitfalls_or_limitations: Index aggregation is dimensionally loose: EDI/EPI are stated as plain (1/3)(A+B+C) sums of quantities with very different units (e.g. Life Expectancy + GDP per capita + Expense %GDP), with normalization only gestured at — the actual scaling of each raw indicator to a comparable range is never shown, which is the crux of whether the cube is meaningful

Sign convention is muddled: states are described as living in a unit cube with ideal diagonal (1,1,1), yet CG components and even axis values go negative (Wellington EPI/SJI negative; cube figures show negative SJI), and forecasts run states above 1.0 (Edinburgh SJI reaches 1.7 by 2047), so the '0-1 normalized cube' framing breaks down in the results

Linear, time-invariant b matrix assumes budget→outcome effects are constant, additive, and instantaneous over a 30-year horizon, ignoring diminishing returns, lags, and saturation — extrapolating ΔS = b·B forward 30 years drives indices well outside any plausible range

OLS fit b_hat = (B_k^T B_k)^{-1} B_k ΔS_k is estimated from only ~7 data points (2009-2015) to fit a 3x7 = 21-parameter matrix, so the system is badly under-determined/overfit and likely unstable (no condition-number or standard errors reported)

Validation is weak: the F-test is stated only as a generic formula with no actual F-values, degrees of freedom, or pass/fail numbers; sensitivity analysis perturbs just one parameter (Gini) on one city, so 'reliable' is asserted more than demonstrated

Forecast monotonically improves for both cities with no equilibrium or feasibility check — the optimizer recommends an ~8x jump in Edinburgh transport share (0.99%→8.0%) with only hand-wavy implementation suggestions

Copy-paste errors in the Wellington plan narrative (items 5-7 repeat Edinburgh's numbers like '1.8227% to 7.9766%'), and the strength/weakness section is mis-numbered as IX/9.1 — small polish slips judges notice

The 50% population analysis is purely qualitative and assumes new residents perfectly mirror current demographic ratios, which conveniently freezes SJI and EDI so only EPI moves — a strong, unverified simplification
reusable_patterns: Geometric metric pattern: when a problem asks you to 'design a metric' from N qualitative pillars, map each pillar to an axis, place the ideal as a diagonal/reference vector, and report (a) cosine similarity = balance/alignment and (b) vector length = magnitude/speed — instantly intuitive and visualizable

Tunable-weight-with-regimes pattern: expose one interpretable parameter (here μ) that trades off two competing objectives, and explicitly enumerate regimes (developed vs developing vs balanced) so graders see policy relevance and you sidestep committing to one weighting
Weakest-link gate: use min(sub-indices) > 0 as a binary classifier for 'sustainable/acceptable vs not', encoding that a system fails on its worst dimension

Reuse-the-metric-as-optimizer: design the evaluation metric so it can also serve as the objective of a constrained optimization (max metric s.t. budget/resource constraints), giving both diagnosis and prescription from one model and tying all sub-tasks together

Estimate-then-forecast loop: fit a linear response matrix (control input → state change) by OLS on historical data, then iterate ΔS = b·B forward to produce multi-decade forecasts — a lightweight, defensible dynamics engine
Answer-mapping in the restatement: explicitly pair each official sub-task with the exact model component that solves it, so judges can checklist coverage
Convert optimizer output to a ranked action list: rank initiatives by |current − optimal| allocation gap to produce a prioritized, cross-case-comparable recommendation table
Honest strengths/weaknesses with named, falsifiable limitations (difference-for-derivative, disaster-blindness, data dependence) — candor reads as maturity and pre-empts criticism
